data.title: Daimler Truck: The Tariff Tug-Of-War Endangers Its Margins
data.description:
Introduction

Tariffs. We have not received any further updates since April 2. The shock was huge, and many have joked about being "liberated" from excessive gains. Economists, politicians, journalists, investors, and all of us have researched, discussed, and formed an initial understanding and what is going on. In these turmoiled waters, I am reviewing all of the industrial stocks I cover, since most of them will, in one way or another, be hit at least by tariffs on imported steel and aluminum.

Cross-border activity surged in March, as importers rushed to transport more imported goods into the U.S. before tariffs kicked in. Trucking played a big role, and this made me examine how Daimler Truck (OTCPK:DTGHF; OTCPK:DTRUY), the largest truck manufacturer in the U.S., could be affected by the new economic environment.

Luckily, Daimler Truck reported its Q4 and FY24 earnings in mid-March, and during the earnings call, it was possible for its management to address the topic of tariffs.

Summary of previous coverage

First of all, a quick summary of what Daimler Truck does and what the state of the art with this business. In late 2021, it was spun off from Mercedes-Benz in late 2021. It owns and produces trucks and buses under the brands Freightliner, Western Star (vocational trucks), FUSO, Mercedes-Benz, BharatBenz, and Thomas Built Buses.

As the company began its stand-alone journey, it immediately targeted boosting its profitability and achieving industry-leading margins above 10%. Among its strengths, Daimler Truck showcases its footprint in North America, where it is the undisputed leader for heavy-duty trucks, with a market share of around 40%. As Daimler Truck benefited from strong order intake during the post-pandemic recovery, I chose to go long, and I held the stock for around a year. Then, I exited it as Daimler Truck saw a deteriorating outlook due to declining orders.

Daimler Truck's FY2024 Results

In 2024, Daimler Truck reported a 12% decrease in unit sales to 460,409 (FY23: 526,053). Total revenues were down 3% to EUR 54.1B (FY23: EUR 55.9B), with EUR 50.7B coming from the industrial business. As we move down the income statement, Daimler Truck saw its margins under pressure, reporting a 31% decline in EBIT to EUR 3.5B (FY23: EUR 5.2B) and a RoS of 6.9% (FY23: 9.4%). Net profit decreased by almost EUR 1B (-23%) to EUR 3.1B.

As we can see, while North America held up rather well, achieving a 12.9% RoS, Mercedes-Benz Trucks (EUR30 and Latin America), and Trucks Asia performed poorly, with sharp declines in sales and margins.

Unfortunately, order intake was down 2% YoY, even though Daimler Truck reported a strong 31% QoQ increase. The company started FY2025 with a book-to-bill ratio of 91%. Generally speaking, we would always like to see a ratio above 100%, but since this is almost impossible due to the cyclical nature of transportation, we see the company is still in good shape. The geography of order intake is, however, mixed: EMEA is down 15% while Latin America is up 47%. In North America, Daimler Truck keeps seeing a freight recession, but its vocational truck division is still enjoying strong momentum.

Now, in March, Daimler Truck reported that in the last few weeks, order intake has been softer due to the ongoing uncertainty. And here we are back to tariffs, which should now be considered as part of the equation that doesn't make this fiscal year look promising for the company.

Daimler Truck's Manufacturing Footprint

Daimler Truck is headquartered in Portland, OR, where manufacturing for Western Star and Freightliner takes place. It also has plants in North Carolina and in South Carolina. It doesn't have operating plants in Canada, but it has several manufacturing sites in Mexico, where Freightliner and heavy-duty trucks are produced, as well as buses, chassis, and other components (engines, electronics, and transmissions).

To this extent, the newly appointed CEO Karin Radstrom explained during the earnings call how Daimler Truck can handle the new hurdles on imports:

It is reasonable to expect that persisting uncertainty around or implementation of tariffs would hurt our volume and profitability projections. The ongoing uncertainty has already impacted order intake in the last few weeks, which might lead to a softer quarter, too [...]

We have assembly factories in the U.S. as well as in Mexico. That's good in the situation we face right now is that we can produce all models in the U.S. or in Mexico. So, for sure, we are preparing ourselves for different scenarios. And we can ramp up more in the U.S. if that would be required. And we're looking at it both from the kind of a shorter-term perspective, how we could increase capacity in the U.S., and also from a longer-term perspective, what we could do.

The good news is that Daimler Truck can produce its models both in the U.S. and in Mexico. Moreover, most of the parts it assembles are USMCA-compliant. So far, therefore, the impact on Daimler Truck has been caused by two events: lower consumer confidence and tariffs on steel and aluminum.

Valuation

Daimler Truck currently trades at a TTM PE of 9. But the fwd PE is around 16. This is rather high, and it shows that the cycle has peaked and that investors are pricing a decline in earnings. This sets the stock in a difficult situation, especially with the ongoing commercial stress caused by tariffs. It is difficult to estimate the impact of these measures on Daimler Truck's earnings, but with a book-to-bill ratio below 100% and news of softening demand, it is hard to imagine that this year Daimler Truck will be able to improve its profitability. I think a 5% RoS would be a reasonable target, but this clearly makes the stock too risky. We should also not be tricked by the juicy 9.6% dividend yield, which is based on the past dividend. Daimler Truck distributes around 40% to 60% of the group's net profit. But with FY24 earnings already down by 23% YoY, I don't see this fiscal year as the year of a rebound. In fact, this year it will pay in May a dividend of EUR 1.90, which is a 5.7% yield. It is still hefty, but we should also consider the German withholding tax of 26% (the stock is listed in Frankfurt).

Conclusion

Freight transportation is the backbone of the economy. With tariffs shielding the American economy, we may be witnessing a redesign in logistics and a shift towards local-for-local strategies. Daimler Truck is in the midst of this change, but since we are in the very early innings, it is hard to predict how the company will sail through turmoiled waters. Analysts seem cautious about the stock, and although Daimler Truck's presence in North America provides some resilience, the unpredictability of manufacturing costs and softer demand suggest this year will be particularly challenging for the company. As a result, I suggest exiting the stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Yext: Banking On New Products, But Customers Continue To Slip
data.description:
Amid hugely volatile markets and global malaise surrounding Trump’s new tariff plan, the operating environment has suddenly become a lot more challenging for smaller-cap companies, especially those with limited resources to navigate sea changes in global trade. And even tech companies that have no exposure whatsoever to manufacturing or imports will be hit, as business investment and marketing cool off.

Yext (NYSE:YEXT), in my view, is caught in a tough storm. The enterprise software company, which is best known for helping its customers manage their digital presence online, has seen relatively calmer trading compared to some of its peers, with its stock down “only” 10% this year. That being said, as the company’s fundamental progress continues in a limping fashion, I fear there are further losses ahead.

I last wrote a sell opinion on Yext in December, when its stock was trading closer to $7 per share. Since then, Yext’s valuation has continued to shrink, which is appropriate alongside its shrinkage in ARR. Bulls had hoped that the company’s recent acquisition of Hearsay Systems, which vaulted Yext back to double-digit total company growth rates, might help to stem the company’s loss of business - but this doesn’t seem to be the case. As such, I’m reiterating my sell opinion on the company.

Now, to Yext’s credit, it does have new products up its sleeves to try to combat the recent declines: its most meaningful upcoming product is a tool called Yext Scout, which is its first major AI-focused release. Yext’s theory is that the new capabilities of AI are further fragmenting the way people navigate and search for information on the web: we use traditional Google searches combined with social media searches. Sometimes, we search using voice instead of web; and often now, we read AI summaries of our searches rather than go into web pages themselves. Yext Scout aims to help companies manage their digital impressions across this newly fragmented web.

It’s possible that Scout will help Yext to reinvigorate discussions with customers and make the company appear more modern. But to me, it’s an older recipe with new branding and a new name. Yext’s founding product, Yext Listings, served a very similar purpose to help companies manage their key information (like opening hours) across various websites. Yext Scout basically upgrades these capabilities to fit with modern search and AI.

What is very telling is the fact that Yext has suspended its full-year revenue guidance, citing an uncertain environment and a hope, though not a guarantee, that it will return to growth in its ARR base. Instead, the company has simply guided to $100-$103 million in adjusted EBITDA for the year, implying strong 49-54% growth in adjusted EBITDA. Wall Street, meanwhile, is expecting 6% growth in revenue to $446.2 million - which to me is ambitious as Yext will start to lap its Hearsay acquisition in August next year.

Amid such shaky prospects, Yext is cheap - though we have to ask ourselves if the value argument is compelling enough to offset Yext’s many risks. At current share prices in the mid-$5s, Yext trades at a market cap of $724.8 million. After we net off the $123.1 million of cash on Yext’s most recent balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $601.7 million. This puts Yext’s valuation multiples at:

  • 1.3x EV/FY25 revenue (based on consensus estimates)
  • 5.9x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA (based on company guidance)

Needless to say that cheapness is a reflection of Yext’s extreme operational risk. In my view, a low valuation and the possibility of major adjusted EBITDA expansion this year are the only true “upside risks” for Yext. But amid a business that has admitted to little visibility over its ability to retain its recurring revenue base, I don’t think Yext is a risk worth bearing. Steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

Q4 download

Let’s now go through Yext’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Yext’s revenue grew 12% y/y to $113.1 million, essentially in-line with Wall Street's expectations of $112.8 million. Revenue growth decelerated one point from 13% y/y in Q3.

Of course, Yext's growth is driven entirely by its acquisition of Hearsay Platforms. In Q3, the company had disclosed that Hearsay contributed to $16.4 million of revenue; in Q4, it stopped providing this specific breakout. But if we assume just flat sequential revenue for Hearsay, we'd arrive at somewhere between a -3% to -4% "organic" growth rate for Yext.

The company's ARR (annualized recurring revenue) base also continues to shrink. The chart below showcases the company's ARR trends by quarter. The company enjoyed a large one-time bump from Hearsay's contribution in Q3 (the company noted that Hearsay came with ~$60 million of ARR), but ARR declined by $6.1 million sequentially versus Q3.

Similarly, the company's net revenue retention rates showcased continued churn. NRR improved slightly to 93% in Q4 versus 92% in Q3, driven in part by Hearsay's consumption-based pricing (where billing is based on actual usage rather than a seat-based subscription that charges a flat rate per head) - but still, the implication that the average customer is reducing its spend by ~7% is daunting.

These figures are unlikely to get any better if the economy heads into a recession. As noted, Yext may not experience any direct, first-order impacts from tariffs: but at the same time, the company's customer base consists of plenty of retail and restaurant clients whose supply chains just got snarled and more expensive. This may discourage new spending (let alone expansions) on software, especially a minor brand like Yext which is struggling against larger brand-name peers.

The only silver lining for Yext is an improvement in profit margins, as adjusted EBITDA grew 66% y/y in nominal terms to $24.6 million, hitting a 21.7% adjusted EBITDA margin: 700bps richer than the year-ago quarter.

Here, Yext is benefiting from multiple rounds of layoffs that have made the company leaner - and its guidance for FY25 suggests that it has another major tranche of margin improvements to go. That being said, Yext's loss of its ARR and its customer base will ultimately be the driver for long-term profitability progress, and continued ARR slippage will drive margin deleverage.

Key takeaways

There is next to zero doubt that Yext is a very cheap stock relative to its peers in the enterprise software sector, where a mid-single digit adjusted EBITDA multiple is unheard of, even after the recent market crash. At the same time, however, enterprise software multiples sit at a premium to the rest of the S&P 500 in large part because of their predictable recurring revenue bases.

Yext, meanwhile, continues to showcase an inability to maintain, let alone grow, its ARR outside of costly acquisitions - thus neatly explaining why the stock has remained so cheap for the past few years. To me, this isn't a bargain that's worth the risks stacked against it.

data.title: A Deep Dive On Lemonade
data.description:
Introduction

In 2024, global non-life and life premium volume reached an estimated $4.6T and $3.0T, respectively, for a grand total of $7.6T in volume. Put differently, the global insurance market is absolutely massive.

However, its sheer size comes with a structural disadvantage: technological inertia. Despite the clear advantages of modernization — artificial intelligence, automation, and big data — the insurance industry is stubbornly resistant to change, clinging to complex systems, bureaucratic processes, and outdated underwriting models.

Many traditional insurers, comforted by centuries-old methods and entrenched systems, have been slow to innovate and adopt new technologies. This has led to low operational efficiency, customer satisfaction, and public trust.

This is where insurtech companies come into play. By leveraging technology, insurtech companies are challenging traditional insurers and disrupting the $7.6T insurance industry, hoping to "claim" a good portion of this gigantic pie.

While there are hundreds, if not thousands, of emerging insurance startups, I want to zoom in on one specific company today: Lemonade (NYSE:LMND).

A few years back, Lemonade was one of the hottest IPOs, sparking huge investor interest as they bet their money on this disruptor, this "next big thing".

As a result, investors bid Lemonade stock from $50 to $180 a share, in just 6 short months. What happened next was a violent 95% selloff in the following two years, driving Lemonade stock down to just $10 a share.

Fortunately, the stock has bounced back recently. At one point, the stock reached $50 for the first time in three years. Today, it trades at about $28 a share, down about 85% from its all-time highs... which begs the question: is Lemonade stock a good buy at current levels?

I covered Lemonade stock about five years ago, and back then, I was quite skeptical about the business. Its sky-high valuation also made me avoid the stock altogether.

But fast-forward to today, I have a completely different view of the company — both from a fundamental and valuation standpoint.

In this deep-dive article, I'll share my update on the company, as well as discuss why investors might want to consider taking a sip of Lemonade.

Company: From Lemons to Lemonade

In 2014, two tech-driven entrepreneurs met through a mutual friend. Through this meeting, Daniel Schreiber and Shai Wininger realized that the insurance industry was primed for disruption given the technological inertia that I mentioned earlier, as well as the negative perceptions surrounding the industry as a whole.

Complex terms, expensive premiums, lengthy claims process, payment delays, lack of personalization, you name it. To the average consumer's eyes, these are... well... lemons.

People want something new, something different, something better.

So, a year later, Schreiber and Wininger decided to make Lemonade out of these lemons.

The duo had the marketing and technical expertise to bring a new product to market. After all, they have a history of starting businesses — Schreiber had co-founded an internet security software company while Wininger had co-founded four companies, including Fiverr (FVRR).

The problem was that both of them had no experience in the insurance industry. While this allowed them to think freely and differently, they still needed someone who understood the ins and outs of the insurance business to build a superior insurance product. As a result, they recruited industry veteran Ty Sagalow as Chief Insurance Officer.

By leveraging technology and its new-found industry expertise, Lemonade became a licensed carrier and subsequently launched a mobile-first insurance platform that would "replace brokers and bureaucracy with bots and machine learning, aiming for zero paperwork and instant everything".

With $13M of seed money, Lemonade finally introduced its first insurance policy in New York in September 2016. Within the first six months, the company signed up more than 14,000 customers.

It was a successful launch, so much so that CEO Daniel Schreiber boasted about how Lemonade was able to process and pay a New Yorker's (Brandon) claim for his stolen Canada Goose Parka — in three seconds.

Between 5:49:07 and 5:49:10, A.I. Jim, Lemonade’s claims bot, reviewed Brandon’s claim, cross-referenced it against his policy, ran 18 anti-fraud algorithms on it, approved it, sent wiring instructions to the bank for the transfer of $729 (Brandon has a $250 deductible), and informed Brandon of the good news.

(CEO Daniel Schreiber — Lemonade Sets a New World Record)

Naturally, Lemonade attracted the interest of several big-time investors, including Softbank, Google, and Allianz. By mid-2019, it had raised a total of $644M over nine funding rounds.

The following year, Lemonade went public, trading under the ticker symbol "LMND".

Fast-forward to today, Lemonade has become one of, if not, the most vertically integrated insurtech companies in the world, making insurance more delightful, affordable, and precise than ever before.

Lemonade's tech stack consists of six key applications, which, collectively, is known as the Customer Cortex:

  • AI Maya: This is Lemonade's onboarding bot, which covers tasks like customer information collection, coverage personalization, and payment facilitation. AI Maya guides customers through the process of purchasing insurance policies — she asks customers simple questions, and based on their responses, provides personalized quotes within seconds. (Fun fact: Maya Prosor, one of Lemonade's founding team members and current Chief Business Officer, is the face of AI Maya.)

  • AI Jim: Serving as Lemonade's claims bot, AI Jim takes first notice of loss from a customer making a claim. It can handle the entire claims process, including data collection, claims approval, and paying the claimant. It can also deny claims and, when human intervention is necessary, escalate them to the customer support team. (Fun Fact 2: Jim Hageman, Lemonade's Former Chief Claims Officer from 2015 to 2022, is the face of AI Jim.)

  • CX.AI: Short for "customer experience, artificial intelligence", CX.AI is Lemonade's bot platform. It leverages natural language processing and machine learning to understand and instantly resolve customer requests with zero human intervention. This includes answering customer coverage questions, updating coverage amounts, and changing payment methods.
  • Cooper: Working behind the scenes, this internal bot handles complex, manual, and repetitive tasks, including processing paper checks, formatting regulatory filings, and even identifying wildfires from real-time satellite images and blocking ads and sales in affected areas.
  • Forensic Graph: Through behavioral economics and machine learning, Forensic Graph predicts, detects, and blocks fraud, helping Lemonade avoid unnecessary potential losses.
  • Blender: This is an insurance management platform designed for Lemonade's internal teams, from customer service, and marketing, to its finance department. For instance, Blender can gather all claims flagged by AI Jim, and provide appropriate next steps to resolve any issues. Another example would be listing, assigning, and paying vendors for certain tasks like assessing damage from, let's say, a car accident.

Fundamentally, the Customer Cortex serves as Lemonade's central operating system, interacting with customers, generating massive amounts of data, and automating various tasks, to create a highly efficient insurance platform capable of delighting customers with top-notch insurance products.

Speaking of which, Lemonade currently offers five different insurance products, namely: renters, homeowners, car, pet, and life insurance.

Launched in 2016, Lemonade's renters and homeowners insurance policies are the first two products offered by the company. These products cover personal property against damage or theft and also provide liability protection for damages or injuries to another person or their property.

The next product that Lemonade launched is pet insurance in July 2020. As of this writing, Lemonade only offers health insurance for cats and dogs, covering "diagnostics, procedures, medication, accidents, vaccinations, therapy, or illness".

The fourth insurance product offered by Lemonade is life insurance, which was launched in the first half of 2021 through a partnership with a third-party carrier. It is a term life insurance, meaning Lemonade will provide coverage for a specific period of time (10 to 40 years). Lemonade will then pay a death benefit if the policyholder passes away during that term.

Last but not least, Lemonade's latest — and probably most exciting — offering is car insurance, which began operations in December 2021. Auto insurance is a completely different ballgame than what Lemonade is used to, so to ensure high product quality while balancing underwriting risk and speed-to-market, Lemonade decided to acquire Metromile, a pay-per-mile car insurance provider, in July 2022.

The $145M Metromile acquisition was an all-stock transaction, and while it diluted Lemonade shareholders, it gave Lemonade the following:

  • Over $155M in cash
  • Over $110M in car premiums
  • A full-stack insurance entity with 49 state licenses
  • Data from 500M car trips

Furthermore, Metromile is not just any regular car insurance company. Like Lemonade, it's an insurtech company that leverages technology to power its entire operation. More specifically, Metromile uses telematics as its main underwriting tool. In simple terms, telematics uses technology to track and analyze how a car is driven (driving habit), how long the car is driven (distance), and where the car is driven (location). This allows Metromile to price its policies based on the individual's driving behavior.

For instance, all else being equal, if the individual drives less than the average driver, then Metromile will charge the individual less. As an example, the monthly price for an individual who drives 100 miles per month will be much lower than one who drives 1,000 miles per month.

In essence, telematics promotes greater fairness and better transparency as customers are charged based on their driving behavior, rather than their "credit score, gender, education, marital status, or other broad-strokes proxies".

This acquisition was an important one for Lemonade as it allowed the company to quickly enter the massive auto insurance industry without going through all the costly headaches associated with launching a new product, let alone an incredibly complex product like car insurance.

Auto insurance is an extremely competitive market, and pushing through its steep and bumpy learning curve can prove treacherous and costly. The most vulnerable time in the life of an insurance product is during its early years, before data accumulate. Being able to minimize or bypass that stage entirely makes for a smoother ride to the top. That means more than getting there faster, it means incurring less risk, and expending less cash, along the way.

(CEO Daniel Schreiber — Why Lemonade Is Acquiring Metromile)

Best of all, Lemonade literally bought Metromile for cents on the dollar at just $145M, as compared to its prior valuation of $1.3B back when it went public via a SPAC merger. Including all the benefits I mentioned above, I think this deal was a steal.

Moving on, encompassing all its insurance products is the Giveback Feature. In a nutshell, this feature donates unclaimed premiums to nonprofits chosen by policyholders. Instead of pocketing leftover money as profits, Lemonade donates it to charitable causes its customers care about, contributing to social good.

This feature does two things for Lemonade:

  • It helps minimize financial volatility as customers are less likely to file a claim as they'll lose the chance to help a nonprofit.
  • It helps maximize trust as customers view Lemonade as a brand that gives back to society, rather than a greedy old traditional insurer.

Because of this feature, Lemonade operates as a certified B Corp, a status that is only awarded to companies that "prove they balance profit with purpose, focusing on people and the planet".

In 2024, Lemonade donated $2.1M through its Giveback Feature.

At the end of the day, Lemonade aims "to become the most loved insurance company in the world". The company will achieve this by combining robotic process automation, behavioral economics, and transparent pricing to deliver insurance in a "smart, instant, and delightful" way.

Ultimately, Lemonade is here to disrupt the centuries-old insurance industry, positioning itself as the next-generation insurance platform for the masses.

The reigning insurers have been on top for centuries, and take in $100-200 billion in revenue each year but longevity shouldn’t be confused with immortality.

Because insurers forged in a bygone era are structurally disadvantaged in the age of the machine.

Their assets turn to liabilities, their culture grows maladapted, and their legacy systems behave like black holes — devouring time and cash — rather than black boxes, making everything faster and more efficient.

Which brings us to Lemonade’s founding thesis.

Lemonade’s founding thesis is that a new kind of insurance company — built on AI and social impact — can delight consumers, collapse costs, and quantify risk like never before.

(Lemonade Investor Day 2024)

Moats: Assessing Lemonade's Durability

According to Morningstar, there are five sources of competitive moats:

  1. Switching costs are obstacles that keep customers from changing between products, like from one company’s product to a competitor’s.
  2. Network effects occur when the value of a good or service increases for both new and existing users as more people use it.
  3. Intangible assets are things such as patents, government licenses, and brand identity that keep a company ahead and competitors at bay.
  4. A company with a cost advantage can produce goods or services at a lower cost, allowing it to undercut its competitors or achieve higher profitability.
  5. Efficient scale benefits companies operating in a market that only supports one or a few competitors, limiting rivalry.

(The Morningstar Economic Moat Rating)

Based on my research and analysis, I believe Lemonade has three out of the five moats above.

Intangible Assets

First, Lemonade has a strong brand moat.

Lemonade has cultivated a robust and distinctive brand identity through its innovative business model, mobile-first approach, and customer-centric system, especially among the younger, tech-driven generation.

Given all the negativity surrounding traditional insurers — overpriced premiums, slow claims process, poor customer experience, etc. — Lemonade has risen to the occasion to offer a refreshed alternative for consumers: insurance that is cheaper, fairer, simpler, sweeter, and quicker.

This earned Lemonade a world-class NPS score of more than 70. As a reference, the average NPS score for the insurance industry is about 35, with some sources claiming it to be as low as 10. A higher NPS score typically signals higher customer loyalty, satisfaction, and support — Lemonade reigns supreme in this regard.

By the same token, Lemonade has been recognized as one of the most loved brands in the insurance industry, boasting a 4.9/5 stars rating on the App Store and a 4.8/5 stars rating on Clearsurance. Most recently, it has been regarded as one of Forbes 2025 America's Best Insurance Companies.

As the Lemonade brand grows, the company will inevitably capture even more market share. Lemonade has proven its ability to scale its longer-tenured products like renters and pet insurance — its newer products like car insurance (and any other future products) will likely follow suit, all thanks to its strong brand presence and high customer satisfaction.

Another evidence of Lemonade's brand moat is the growth of its customer base. As you can see, Lemonade's customer count continues to grow with each passing quarter, ending Q4 with 2.4M Total Customers, which is up 20% YoY. While growth has slowed down compared to a few years ago, its growth has been accelerating over the past few quarters, signifying a pick-up in brand awareness and engagement. I'll cover this in more detail in the Growth section.

That said, how was Lemonade able to attract so many customers (2.4M Customers) in such a short time (less than 10 years)?

I believe price is the largest driving factor to Lemonade's growth. Across virtually all its products, Lemonade offers one of, if not, the cheapest rates in the market.

Take renters insurance for instance. Lemonade has an industry-low average cost of $12/month, which is about 40% lower than the average rate of $20/month.

How about car insurance? Lemonade's minimum coverage and full coverage policies, on average, cost about $40/month and $66/month, respectively, which is about 43% and 54% cheaper than the industry average.

Offering customers such low prices is what sets Lemonade apart, driving a flood of customers into the platform. This is what management meant by offering "killer prices".

The question is: if Lemonade can offer these price points, why can't competitors do the same?

The answer lies in the next moat...

Cost Advantage

The second moat that Lemonade possesses is cost advantage.

The primary difference between Lemonade and legacy insurers is their structures.

While incumbents built their businesses around agents, paperwork, and bureaucracy, Lemonade built its business around technology, AI, and bots.

From day one, we sought out to build a different type of company. One that has less people and more code.

(COO Adina Eckstein — Lemonade 2024 Investor Day)

I've already explained Lemonade's Customer Cortex in the previous section, but let me give you a few numbers to paint a picture of how much "technology" runs the Lemonade platform.

According to Lemonade:

  • AI Maya sells ~98% of Lemonade's policies, as compared to incumbents where they have ~95% of their policies sold by human agents.
  • AI Jim takes first notice of loss for ~98% of claims and handles 55% of claims, end-to-end, without human intervention.
  • CX.AI instantly resolves about one-third of customer requests, automatically.
  • Cooper saves ~10,000 human hours by constantly monitoring servers, handling repetitive tasks, and pushing over 12,000 software builds a year.

Lemonade's structural advantage also comes from a decade of building its tech stack — a single, vertically integrated proprietary system built in-house. In contrast, legacy insurers depend heavily on third-party vendors and acquisitions for data and technology, which leads to a clutter of different systems mashed together.

Creating a seamless AI powered experience that delivers personalized customer interactions, improves efficiency, and drives better underwriting and pricing, require then, a unified full stack system with AI at its core. Unlike traditional insurers, the Lemonade platform was designed, built, and maintained in-house and I believe this is our secret weapon.

(CTO Shai Wininger — Lemonade FY2024 Q3 Earnings Call)

This structural advantage leads to cost advantage — and it's beginning to take shape in Lemonade's financials. Here are some examples:

Lemonade's pet business has scaled from $63M in 2021 to $283M in 2024 while its Gross Loss Ratio decreased from 98% to 69% and Cost per Claim collapsed from $44 to $19.

Since 2021, Lemonade has grown In-Force Premium (IFP) by a 25% CAGR while employee headcount only grew at a 2% CAGR.

Similarly, Lemonade's Number of Customers per Employee increased by 25% from ~1,600 in 2023 to ~2,000 in 2024. How about legacy insurers? Well... they average around 300 Customers per Employee, and they were flat YoY. In other words, Lemonade's Customers per Employee is over six times higher than legacy insurers, and what's more, it's still improving. To me, that is some serious structural advantage right there.

There are more examples I can give out, but you get the point. By leveraging AI, Lemonade can drastically reduce labor costs and deliver more precise, superior customer experiences while collecting far more data than any human agent can possibly do.

Because of this cost advantage, Lemonade can offer killer prices without damaging its balance sheet. What's more, Lemonade is still a young company that has barely scaled — the company has a lot more efficiency gains to extract in the coming years.

As Lemonade scales further, it can pass on all the excess savings to customers, providing even better value to them. As management mentioned, every 10 percentage point change in price can yield a 50% to 150% change in conversion.

Just imagine how big the Lemonade platform can get when it fully exercises its cost advantage moat and drives prices lower.

Switching Costs

Killer prices drive customers to try Lemonade, but it's the technology that keeps them loyal for the long haul.

Once customers try the Lemonade experience, I think they are more inclined to stay as customers than to switch to another provider.

Think about it: with rock-bottom prices in place, a wonderfully designed mobile app, and a seamlessly instantaneous claims process, why would customers leave Lemonade and sign up with a traditional insurer, which is typically more expensive, less user-friendly, and much slower?

Perhaps, they would leave because Lemonade's insurance does not have enough coverage. This could be true since Lemonade focuses on offering basic coverage, thus its lower price points. But as Lemonade scales and grows its customer base and collects more data, its technology and AI will become even smarter and more efficient, thus amplifying its cost advantage moat. This allows Lemonade to expand its coverage offerings while maintaining its already-low prices, which ultimately makes Lemonade insurance a superior product compared to incumbents. So this problem of having insurance policies that are "too basic" should eventually be alleviated as Lemonade matures.

Another reason why customers might leave Lemonade is due to the absence of live agents. This is a double-edged sword for Lemonade — while Lemonade relies on bots to automatically onboard new clients, process claims, and answer customer inquiries, the lack of human interaction may be frustrating for some. Yet again, I'm sure the majority of customers prefer to get their claims paid out instantly, instead of waiting for days, weeks, and months to get their claims approved and sent to them, which, by the way, usually involves numerous back-and-forth with an agent.

I understand that having a wide enough coverage and access to human agents are important factors for some policyholders but for the overwhelming majority of people who just want basic coverage at killer prices, I think staying on the Lemonade platform is a no-brainer.

In addition, as customers remain longer with Lemonade, they are more likely to adopt more products under the Lemonade umbrella. Multi-product adoption benefits Lemonade in two ways:

  1. Increases premium per customer. For instance, in 2024, 17% of Lemonade's pet insurance policies were cross-sold to existing customers, leading to a 3.7x increase in median premium per customer despite minimal incremental customer acquisition costs.
  2. Increases switching costs for customers. Bundling multiple insurance offerings typically saves them more money, as opposed to having different insurance products across multiple insurance carriers. This makes them even more stubborn to leave Lemonade, thus enhancing platform stickiness.

We can see that Lemonade's switching cost moat is getting stronger over time. Annual Dollar Retention, defined as the percentage of In-Force Premium retained over the last twelve months, was 86% as of Q4 of 2024, an increase from 81% in Q1 of 2021. This is despite Lemonade deliberately not renewing certain policies that failed to meet its underwriting criteria over the last few quarters.

I expect this metric to reach 90%+ in the long term. That said, the company's overall ADR trend is up, signifying a strengthening of its switching cost moat.

All in all, Lemonade's brand, cost advantage, and switching cost moats should help the company gain market share, ultimately driving sustainable, profitable growth for many years to come.

Growth: Squeezing Out the Competition

Before we dive into Lemonade's financials, let me briefly talk about Lemonade's business model.

At the core of Lemonade’s business model is reinsurance. Basically, reinsurance is "insurance for insurance companies". Under a reinsurance contract, Lemonade transfers, or "cedes", a percentage of the premium to reinsurers. In return, these reinsurers agree to pay Lemonade a "ceding commission" as well as cover a portion of the corresponding claims.

Here's a simplified chart of how reinsurance works:

Initially, Lemonade cedes 75% of the premium to reinsurers, while retaining 25% of the premium for operating expenses and potential profits. The higher the percentage it cedes to reinsurers, the more "protected" the business is from potential losses, but this consequently lowers its Revenue and earnings potential.

Over the years, Lemonade's underwriting and risk management models have gotten smarter and better, which allowed Lemonade to reduce its quota share protection to just 55% as of July 2024. This also means that Lemonade gets to keep the remaining 45% of the premium, thus increasing its earnings potential.

Fundamentally, buying reinsurance serves two purposes for Lemonade:

  • Maximize Capital Efficiency: in most jurisdictions, insurers are required to keep significant capital reserves to withstand periods of elevated levels of claims due to, for instance, natural disasters. By sharing premiums, Lemonade shifts most of the capital reserve requirement to the reinsurer, thus allowing Lemonade to stay relatively capital-light.
  • Optimize Gross Margin: ceding a portion of its premiums to reinsurers reduces bottom-line volatility as reinsurers, not Lemonade, have to cover most of the claims. At the same time, Lemonade has also signed reinsurance agreements to reduce the maximum amount that Lemonade has to pay for any one claim, thus shielding Lemonade from extreme losses.

In short, Lemonade's reinsurance model allows it "to architect our business to be at once capital-light and possessed of a predictable and growing gross margin".

Also worth mentioning is that Lemonade operates a subscription-based model, which creates a highly recurring, high-quality income stream for the business.

Having said that, here are the different components that make up Lemonade's Revenue:

  • Net Earned Premium: This represents the earned portion of Lemonade's Gross Written Premium minus the earned portion of the premium ceded to reinsurers.
  • Ceding Commission Income: This is the commission Lemonade receives from reinsurers for ceding a portion of its Gross Written Premium. This income compensates Lemonade for the costs of acquiring customers and underwriting policies.
  • Net Investment Income: This is interest income generated from investing in securities like bonds, Treasury bills, or money market funds. Insurance companies typically use "float" — premiums collected from policyholders that are sitting idle on the balance sheet before they are used to pay out claims or other obligations — to invest in securities to generate investment income.
  • Commission and Other Income: This consists of commission earned from placing policies with third-party insurers and fees collected from policyholders relating to installment premiums.

With these in mind, let's dive into Lemonade's financials, starting with growth.

Looking at the company's topline, In Force Premium — or the total annualized premium for all active policies — was $944M as of Q4, up 26% YoY. This was slightly ahead of management's guidance of $938M to $942M set at the beginning of 2024, reflecting the strength and predictability of Lemonade's business model.

While it is nowhere near pre-2023 growth rates, it is still encouraging to see IFP growth accelerating in the last few quarters, pointing to a recovery in business momentum and increased demand for Lemonade's insurance products.

Breaking it down by product type, Lemonade's $944M of IFP consists of:

  • Homeowners insurance of $503M, up 16% YoY
  • Pet insurance of $283M, up 57% YoY.
  • Car insurance of $122M, up 7% YoY.
  • European insurance of $24M, up 140% YoY
  • Other insurance of $12M, up 33% YoY.

Of the three core products, we can see that Pet is growing the fastest, followed by the legacy Homeowners segment. Car, on the other hand, has been slow to gain traction. However, we should see the Car segment step on the gas pedal (pun intended) as Lemonade continues to expand operations nationwide. Just recently, Lemonade Car expanded to Colorado, where people spend $7B on car insurance annually. This also brings Lemonade's exposure to approximately 40% of the US car insurance market — still a lot of room to grow.

Moving forward, we can expect IFP growth to continue to accelerate in Q1. For one, management recently announced that Lemonade has crossed $1B of IFP, which has surpassed their Q1 guidance of $997M to $1B. This equates to a 26% YoY growth over Q1 of 2024, but since this was announced on March 25th (one week before Q1 closes), we will likely see Q1 IFP to the tune of $1.02B, which is about a 28% YoY growth.

For the full year, management expects an IFP of $1.203B to $1.208B, representing a 28% YoY growth. Beyond that, management expects "sustained acceleration towards our cruising velocity above 30%, expected in 2026". In other words, we can expect robust IFP growth ahead for at least the next two or three years.

That said, IFP growth was largely driven by higher customer count. In Q4, Total Customers were about 2.4M, up 20% YoY. Improved monetization also contributed to IFP growth. In Q4, Premium Per Customer was up 5% YoY to $388 due to increased multi-product adoption, growth in average policy value, and a continued mix shift toward higher value policies.

The number of Customers on a platform is probably the most important metric to track as it is a leading indicator of future IFP growth, and subsequently, Revenue growth. Thus, seeing Lemonade continue to grow its customer base with each passing quarter is a testament to its strong value proposition and increasing Revenue potential.

On that note, Q4 Revenue was $149M, up 29% YoY with growth accelerating 10pp QoQ. This was driven by higher IFP, which consequently led to robust growth across all of Lemonade's Revenue segments.

  • Net Earned Premium of $101M, up 21% YoY.
  • Ceding Commission Income of $30M, up 61% YoY.
  • Net Investment Income of $9M, up 32% YoY.
  • Commission and Other Income of $9M, up 41% YoY.

In terms of the Revenue outlook, management expects Q1 Revenue of $143M to $145M, implying a 22% YoY growth. If management meets this guidance, it means that Revenue is set to decline on a QoQ basis from $149M in Q4. Correct me if I'm wrong, but this will also mark the first quarter ever since the company went public, where Revenue declined on a sequential basis.

This was a head-scratcher for me because management guided for a sequential increase in IFP and Gross Earned Premium, which should theoretically lead to an expansion in Revenue. However, management is expecting Revenue to decline, which also invalidates Lemonade's "highly recurring and naturally growing Revenue streams".

I think two factors might have led to this phenomenon: 1) the intentional removal of higher-risk homeowners policies from its book, and 2) the unfavorable timing of Ceding Commission Income paid to Lemonade.

That said, analysts expect Q1 Revenue of $145M, which is the high end of management's guidance, but do consider the fact that Lemonade has a 100% track record of beating analyst Revenue estimates ever since it went public. Given management's strong execution and Lemonade's recent IFP milestone of $1B, I believe Q1 Revenue will come in ahead of management's guidance — I'd say at least $150M.

For the full year, management expects 2025 Revenue of $655M to $657M, implying a 25% YoY growth at the midpoint.

Whatever it is, Lemonade has a massive growth runaway ahead as it continues to disrupt the legacy insurance industry. Here's how Lemonade can grow further:

  1. Acquiring first-time buyers: According to Lemonade, 92% of its home and renters customers did not switch to Lemonade from another carrier — meaning, they are first-time insurance buyers. Lemonade clearly resonates with the price-sensitive, underserved population, which should help the company capture this demographic for years to come.
  2. Cross-selling: As a household grows, customers will inevitably need additional coverage, whether it be for a new home, car, or pet. By tapping its existing user base, Lemonade can cross-sell its product at minimal cost. This applies to new insurance products as well. For instance, of the ~2.4M Lemonade customers, about 1.2M of them already have car insurance with another carrier. Assuming an average annual premium of $2,000 per customer (Lemonade's Car Premium Per Customer is currently $1,800), it would translate to $2.4B of incremental car premiums at no additional acquisition costs.
  3. Expand Geographically: As I've mentioned earlier, Lemonade Car is available in only 40% of the US car insurance market, so Lemonade can gradually expand to other states to increase its growth exposure. Furthermore, Lemonade is still a tiny business in Europe, with only $24M of IFP as of Q4.
  4. Partnerships: Expanding its partner network could also boost Lemonade's brand awareness and Revenue growth. For instance, partnering with ride-hailing companies like Uber and Lyft could supercharge Lemonade's growth.

Without a doubt, car insurance will serve as the reactive agent that will propel the Lemonade business to new levels — one that will 10x the business in the next decade or so, to $10B of IFP.

But consider this as well. Lemonade has 2.4M Customers. The US has 340M people. Let's say there are 250M adults in the US. That means Lemonade has a market penetration rate of less than 1%.

Given Lemonade's strong brand and ability to scale some of its earlier products, namely pet and renters to 5% to 8% market share, I believe Lemonade has what it takes to squeeze out the competition and 10x its business over the long run — and maybe more.

It will surely be a long and difficult road, but it will be a prize worth fighting for.

The world spends more money on insurance than on advertising, aerospace, agriculture, artificial intelligence, automotive, cloud-computing, cybersecurity, defense, education, electronics, entertainment, fashion & apparel, food & beverages, oil & gas, renewable energy, pharma, semiconductors, shipping, software, sports, telecom, tourism, transportation,

In short: Insurance is a prize worth fighting for.

(Lemonade Investor Day 2024)

Profitability: Getting Sweeter

Lemonade's cost structure consists of five main components. The first two make up the company's Cost of Revenue, while the last three make up its Operating Expenses.

  • Loss and Loss Adjustment Expense, Net
  • Other Insurance Expense
  • Sales & Marketing
  • Research & Development
  • General & Administrative

Growth makes little sense if Lemonade does not have an efficient cost structure, and thus, is unable to turn a profit. Although Lemonade is still unprofitable at the moment, the company is well on track to achieving durable profitable growth.

For one, Lemonade is gaining significant economies of scale, as evidenced by its improved unit economics.

In Q4, Gross Profit was $64M, up 90% YoY, with Gross Margin expanding 14pp YoY and 16pp QoQ, to an all-time high of 43%. This was mainly driven by higher IFP and improved loss ratios.

Likewise, Adjusted Gross Profit — Gross Profit excluding Net Investment Income, Interest Income, and Other Income, but adds back some non-direct expenses — was $66M, up 88% YoY. In essence, Adjusted Gross Profit is the money left after growing its core insurance business and paying variable costs.

Adjusted Gross Profit as a % of Gross Earned Premium was 29% in Q4, improving 9pp YoY and 11pp QoQ. Gross Earned Premium is used as the denominator (instead of Gross Written Premium) as it excludes the impact of premiums ceded to reinsurers, thus reflecting Lemonade-only volume and the unit economics from that volume. As you can see below, Lemonade's Adjusted Gross Profit as a % of Gross Earned Premium is at an all-time high, indicating higher earnings potential.

Another way of examining Lemonade's unit economics is to look at its loss ratios. The lower they are, the more profitable Lemonade is — impressively, Lemonade's loss ratios are at an all-time low.

  • Q4 Gross Loss Ratio — defined as the ratio of Losses and Loss Adjustment Expense to Gross Earned Premium — was 63%, down 14pp YoY.
  • Q4 Net Loss Ratio — defined as the ratio of Losses and Loss Adjustment Expense, less amounts ceded to reinsurers, to Net Earned Premium — was 62%, down 16pp YoY.

For context, large insurers have loss ratios in the range of 60% to 80% so that puts Lemonade as one of the top performers in the industry. Moreover, Lemonade's loss ratios are still on a downward trajectory, which means that Lemonade's underwriting models are getting smarter and more efficient, and over time, I see the potential for its loss ratios to fall below 60%, thus significantly outperforming even the largest and most experienced insurers in the game. This will further solidify Lemonade's cost advantage moat.

So how was Lemonade able to consistently improve its loss ratios over the last few quarters? According to management, the following factors have contributed to improving loss ratios:

  • Diversification: geographic and product diversification helps Lemonade manage CAT exposure, thus minimizing losses.
  • Not renewing certain policies: management has deliberately taken steps to stop renewing certain CAT-exposed homeowners policies that do not meet its AI-powered underwriting standards. While this reduces Lemonade's IFP growth, it significantly improves Lemonade's loss ratios.
  • Newer verticals maturing: At the beginning, newer verticals tend to exhibit higher loss ratios, but as time goes by, loss ratios from these verticals will eventually fall. Take the pet vertical (launched in 2020) for example. As you can see below, as Lemonade Pet IFP increases, its Gross Loss Ratio has gone nowhere but down, from 98% in 2021 to just 69% in 2024.

The improvements in Lemonade's unit economics reflect the scalability of its business model and its potential to drive serious earnings in the coming decades. This is even more impressive considering that Lemonade offers the lowest price in the industry. Over time, Lemonade's digital business architecture will drive accelerating returns while traditional insurers struggle with diminishing returns.

But first, Lemonade needs to scale.

Management understands this, which is why they have ramped up spending to drive further growth — more specifically, through a growth funding program called Synthetic Agents.

Launched in July 2023, the Synthetic Agents program is "a novel financial structure that unlocks growth without depleting cash". In essence, Lemonade has partnered with leading venture firm General Catalyst (GC) to fund 80% of Lemonade's Sales and Marketing Growth spend, and in return, Lemonade will pay back the principal borrowed plus a commission of up to 16% of the premiums GC helped to finance. Once fully paid, Lemonade will retain all future income from the customers acquired from the Synthetic Agents program.

This program has a couple of benefits for Lemonade. It allows Lemonade to accelerate growth without:

  • depleting cash levels, thus protecting its balance sheet, and
  • issuing more equity, thus preventing shareholder dilution.

Initially, GC will finance up to $150M of Lemonade's growth efforts, but this deal has since been extended twice for another $140M in January 2024 and an additional $200M in its recent Q4 report. Safe to say, both parties are happy with the outcome of the program.

Keep in mind that the Growth spend funded by GC will be reflected in Lemonade's income statement, under Sales & Marketing Expenses, which will be dilutive to Net Income. On the other hand, it will also be reflected on the cash flow statement, under Proceeds from Borrowings Under Financing Agreement, which will be accretive to Financing Cash Flow, and thus, Cash levels.

With that said, as a result of the Synthetic Agents program, Lemonade's Sales & Marketing Expenses spiked 95% YoY to $48M. Operating Expenses excluding Loss and Loss Adjustment Expense, Net, was $124M, up 28% YoY. As a reminder, IFP grew 26% YoY. In other words, Lemonade's topline is growing slower than its expense line, which is not good.

However, if we remove Growth spend (mainly funding from GC) from the equation, we can see that Lemonade is driving considerable IFP growth while Operating Expenses excluding Growth spend have barely changed. This is an extreme display of operating leverage.

I understand what management is trying to convey: Lemonade has a scalable business model that doesn't require a marginal increase in fixed costs. But in my view, not including Growth spend in Operating Expenses is misleading because growth would have been much lower without the Synthetic Agents program.

Growth spend, whether borrowed or not, is still an expense, and therefore, it still needs to be taken into consideration in the company’s profitability analysis. With this in mind, I prefer to analyze Lemonade's financials by including the Growth spend funded by GC.

Moving down the income statement, Adjusted EBITDA was $(24)M in Q4. If we exclude the $36M of Growth spend in Q4, Lemonade would have turned Adjusted EBITDA positive for the first time ever — but again, it's a disservice to exclude Growth spend from the profit equation.

Regardless, we can see that Lemonade is driving significant operating leverage, with Adjusted EBITDA Margins improving 9pp YoY and 20pp QoQ, to (16)% as of Q4.

We can see the same thing for Net Income as well, which was $(30)M, with Margins improving 17pp YoY and 30pp QoQ to (20)%.

But on a less positive note, expect profitability to take a slight hit in Q1, mainly due to the California Wildfires. In Q1, management expects Adjusted EBITDA of $(49)M to $(46)M, implying an Adjusted EBITDA Margin of (33)%.

This guidance includes a ~$20M impact from the wildfires. This figure might look large, but it could've been larger if not for Lemonade's reinsurance contracts — gross losses from the wildfires were estimated to be $45M but only $20M will impact the bottom line as a portion of those losses will be ceded to reinsurers.

For the full year, management expects Adjusted EBITDA of $(140)M to $(135)M, implying a (21)% Margin, so expect margins to improve after Q1 and beyond.

This guidance also includes approximately $165M of Growth spend in 2025, so technically, Adjusted EBITDA would have been positive without this Growth spend.

That said, despite the California Wildfires and a 40% YoY increase in Growth spend, Lemonade is expected to continue to deliver operational efficiencies in 2025, eventually exiting 2026 with positive Adjusted EBITDA.

Keep in mind that Lemonade achieved all these profitability improvements despite one of the most challenging macroeconomic conditions, characterized by elevated inflation and numerous catastrophes.

For instance, over the last 45 years, 2023 (purple line) and 2024 (orange line) were the two years with the most number of individual weather and climate disasters in the US with at least $1B in damages, with 28 and 27 events, respectively. To put this into perspective, the 1980-2023 annual average (black line) is just 9 events.

And yet, Lemonade's margins continue to improve. Sure, there will be dips in profits along the way (as exemplified by the wildfires in Q1), but over a long period, Lemonade continues to demonstrate strong operating leverage.

This speaks volumes about the durability, stability, and scalability of Lemonade's business model.

  • Durability to withstand catastrophes.
  • Stability to minimize margin volatility.
  • Scalability to improve earnings.

Back in 2021, I was skeptical about Lemonade's business model and its ability to turn a profit. However, its recent performance proves otherwise.

Lemonade's profitability metrics are getting sweeter by the day.

Through the combination of a delightful brand, killer prices, highly-recurring Revenue streams, a reinsurance construct, and an ever-improving AI, Lemonade is able to scale its business rapidly while driving loss ratios down — altogether, they form Lemonade's secret recipe for long-term profitable growth.

Health: Cash Flow Positivity Marks a New Era

As of Q4, Lemonade has $1.0B of Cash and Investments:

  • Cash and Cash Equivalents were $376M. As you can see below, this figure has been expanding over the last few quarters.
  • Total Investments were $635M, consisting primarily of fixed maturity securities that generate Lemonade's Net Investment Income.

Factoring in Total Debt of $107M, Lemonade has a Net Cash position of $904M. To put this into perspective, Lemonade's Net Cash position represents nearly 50% of its current Market Cap of $2.0B!

Lemonade also maintains a risk-based capital ratio of more than 500%, which is well above risk-based capital standards and other minimum capital and surplus requirements — a ratio of more than 200% implies "an acceptably low expectation of becoming financially impaired". Anything below this threshold would trigger some level of regulatory intervention.

The lower the percentage, the more severe the regulatory response, including, in the event of a mandatory control level event (total adjusted capital falls below 70% of the insurer's authorized control level risk-based capital), placing the insurance company into receivership. As of December 31, 2024, our risk-based capital ratio was 577% for LIC and 525% for MIC.

(Lemonade FY2024 10-K)

That said, I expect Lemonade's Net Cash position to expand over time, as Lemonade has just turned Free Cash Flow positive. In Q4, FCF was $11M, representing a FCF Margin of 7%, which improved 32pp YoY. As you can see, this is the second straight quarter of positive FCF.

Management also shared Adjusted FCF, which adds back Net Borrowings under the Synthetic Agents program. In Q4, Adjusted FCF was $27M, representing an 18% Margin, which improved 28pp YoY. For the full year, 2024 Adjusted FCF was $48M. Moving forward, management expects their second consecutive year of positive Adjusted FCF in 2025.

Again, I'm not a big fan of adding back borrowed money from the Synthetic Agents program as:

  1. FCF would have been much lower without growth funding from GC.
  2. It is still an expense that will eventually be paid back to GC.

The important thing here is that Lemonade has turned FCF positive, a strong signal of a viable business model with ongoing operating leverage.

In the first nine years as a company, Lemonade has financed its operations mainly through private and public equity offerings, diluting shareholders in the process.

Fundamentally, being FCF-positive marks a new era of true profit generation and shareholder value creation. In the long run, incremental FCF/share growth will be accretive to the company's valuation.

Valuation: Sip or Skip?

Let me preface this section by saying that it is challenging to value Lemonade stock, given the company's small size and unprofitability. As such, I'll be using a few methods to gauge the company's valuation and how attractive it is from a risk-to-reward standpoint.

First, let's look at valuation multiples.

As it stands, Lemonade trades at a Price to Sales multiple of 3.7x, which is a significant discount to its peak multiple of 107x. It is also trading close to its bottom multiple of 1.8x. So from a historical standpoint, Lemonade stock looks very cheap.

We can see the same thing for its Price to Book multiple — a more traditional metric to value insurance companies — which is at about 3.4x as of this writing. Again, the ratio is well below its peak multiple of 19x, implying relative undervaluation.

If we compare its multiple to some of its larger peers, we can see that Lemonade is trading at a premium to its peer group. Aside from Progressive (PGR), Lemonade is trading at a premium — this is justified given Lemonade's higher growth rate and growth potential. After all, these are the companies that Lemonade is currently disrupting.

On the other hand, Lemonade is trading at a significant premium to other insurtech companies, some of which have much higher growth rates than Lemonade. Therefore, Lemonade may not offer the best value if you want exposure to insurtech companies.

On a side note, institutions have been loading up on Lemonade stock, which may offer some validation for investors.

On the other side, insider ownership is particularly low for a small-cap growth company, with both co-founders still leading the company. As of this writing, CEO Daniel Schreiber owns only 0.07% of the company, while CTO Shai Wininger owns about 4.65%. Collectively, insiders own 5.48% of Lemonade stock — not exactly a high enough figure that reflects skin in the game.

I've also done a DCF analysis to determine Lemonade's intrinsic value per share, but keep in mind that I'm taking an unconventional approach here — I'll be using Adjusted EBITDA as a proxy for Cash Flow since management did not provide their long-term FCF Margin target.

During Lemonade's Investor Day, management provided the following "simplified approach to valuation", highlighting that they expect the company to grow Gross Earned Premium by 30% annually for the next 7 years. They also applied a 12% Adjusted EBITDA Margin against Gross Earned Premium, which, I assume, would be their long-term margin target.

As a result, management arrived at an intrinsic value per share of $90+.

Now, management has guided for a $1B Gross Earned Premium in 2025. Assuming Lemonade has reached optimal margins today, a 12% Margin will get us an Adjusted EBITDA of $120M.

Management also guided for $656M of Revenue in 2025 — an Adjusted EBITDA of $120M translates to an Adjusted EBITDA Margin as a % of Revenue of about 18%. This will be Lemonade's long-term Adjusted EBITDA Margin in my DCF model.

With that being said, here are my key assumptions:

  • FY2034 Revenue of $3.6B. I have used consensus estimates for the first three years. After that, I expect growth to decelerate to just 15% by 2032. Remember that management expects Gross Earned Premium to grow at a CAGR of 30% for the next 7 years, so my assumption is actually very conservative.
  • FY2034 Adjusted EBITDA Margin of 18%, as explained earlier. Per management, they expect Adjusted EBITDA to turn positive by the end of 2026, so I'm projecting positive margins after 2026, eventually reaching an optimal Adjusted EBITDA Margin of 18%.
  • FY2034 "Cash Flow" of $512M at a 14% Margin. The formula for "Cash Flow" is Adjusted EBITDA minus Income Tax and Capital Expenditures.

Based on a Perpetual Growth Rate of 3% and a Discount Rate of 12%, I get an intrinsic value per share of $50 for Lemonade stock, which is nearly double its current price of $28. This is the base case.

Again, I'm using a simplified and unconventional DCF method, so take my valuation estimate with a grain of salt.

As a reference, analysts have an average price target of $32 a share, with a street-low of $15 and a street-high of $60.

Be that as it may, despite being down 85% from its all-time highs, Lemonade, as a business, is stronger than it has ever been. Compared to a few years ago, Lemonade is a much larger business with: 1) more product verticals, 2) accelerating growth, 3) loss ratios below its target range of 70%, 4) a clear path towards GAAP profitability, and 5) cash flow positivity.

What's more, following the recent 50% selloff, Lemonade now trades near the bottom of its valuation multiple range, with nearly 50% of its Market Cap being Net Cash. Alongside improving fundamentals, I think Lemonade stock offers decent upside potential with a good margin of safety.

Risks

  • Competition: Insurance is a highly competitive space. This includes more established companies like GEICO, Progressive, and State Farm, which have strong brand reputations, higher financial ratings, and better bundling options. In addition, the rise of autonomous vehicles and robotaxis may disrupt the car insurance industry, thus jeopardizing Lemonade's Car business.
  • Catastrophes: Insurance companies experience significant fluctuations in their operating results due to natural disasters. While Lemonade's reinsurance contracts soften its losses from any particular catastrophic event, they do not completely negate the losses incurred. As such, Lemonade's business fundamentals and stock price will likely fluctuate as unpredictably as the weather.
  • Unprofitability: As discussed earlier, Lemonade is still unprofitable on a GAAP basis, which may continue to pressure the stock until the company turns a profit. This is especially true in this uncertain market environment where investors are likely to engage in a flight to quality, moving their assets to more profitable, stable, and defensive investments.
  • Tariffs: As you know, we are currently in the middle of a tariff war, which indirectly impacts the insurance industry. For instance, tariffs raise the price of imported goods such as timber and cars, which lead to higher claims costs for insurers, thus lowering overall profitability. While I believe higher tariffs will benefit Lemonade as more customers look for cheaper options, increased claims costs as a result of these tariffs could further delay Lemonade's profitability goals.
  • High Short Interest: Lemonade has a Short Ratio of more than 20%, so skepticism is quite high. While a high Short Ratio sets the stage for a possible short squeeze, it could point to underlying concerns about the company’s fundamentals — in Lemonade's case, it's probably unprofitability.

Thesis

Lemonade is redefining insurance, disrupting a centuries-old industry by leveraging reinsurance, AI, and killer prices.

Over the last decade, Lemonade has built itself strong competitive moats — brand, cost advantage, and switching costs — that position the company well for robust Revenue and earnings growth for many years to come.

Compared to a few years ago, IFP has more than quadrupled, loss ratios have come down materially, and FCF has turned positive. Despite the improvement in business fundamentals, Lemonade stock is still down 85% from its peak and 50% from its IPO price.

At 50% Net Cash and trading at about 3x its sales and book value, Lemonade stock offers decent value for investors who want exposure to a disruptive insurtech company.

As I write this, the stock market is going through a major sell-off — and Lemonade has not been spared. Perhaps, this could be a good time to take a sip of Lemonade.
